docs.title: INFTY Documentation
docs.text:
INFTY is an optimization-centric toolkit for Continual AI. It provides plug-and-play optimizers and visualization tools for diagnosing optimization behavior in continual-learning systems.

The library is designed for research workflows where users need to replace or augment an existing PyTorch optimizer without rewriting the whole training pipeline.

docs.title: Documentation map
docs.text: Page Purpose Installation Install INFTY from PyPI or from source. Quick Start Run the smallest optimizer examples and the PILOT demo. User Guide Understand optimizer families, closures, continual-learning workflows, and visualization utilities. API Reference Look up public classes, functions, arguments, return values, and expected closure contracts. Examples Find runnable examples for C-Flat, ZeroFlow, UniGrad-FS, visualization, and PILOT integration. Developer Guide Extend the library with new optimizers, plots, examples, and documentation. Architecture Understand INFTY's module layout and optimizer wrapper design. Troubleshooting Resolve common installation, closure, training, and plotting issues. Citation Cite INFTY and related algorithm papers.

docs.title: Main concepts
docs.text:
INFTY follows four design ideas:

  1. Optimizer wrapping: most INFTY optimizers wrap a standard PyTorch optimizer such as torch.optim.SGD or torch.optim.Adam.
  2. Closure-based training: the training loss is supplied through a closure that returns (logits, loss_list).
  3. Continual-learning awareness: several optimizers explicitly handle flatness, gradient conflicts, or zeroth-order updates, which are common concerns in continual learning.
  4. Diagnostics as first-class utilities: the package includes tools for loss landscapes, Hessian ESD, gradient-conflict curves, and trajectory visualization.

docs.title: Recommended reading order
docs.text:
New users should read:

  1. Installation
  2. Quick Start
  3. User Guide

Users integrating INFTY into an existing training framework should additionally read:

  1. API Reference
  2. Examples
  3. Troubleshooting

Contributors should read:

  1. Developer Guide
  2. Architecture
  3. Roadmap

docs.title: Current status
docs.text: INFTY is an alpha-stage research library. The core optimizer interfaces are usable, but some APIs may evolve as new continual-learning use cases are added. For experiments intended for publication, record the exact INFTY version or commit hash.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: This reference documents the public interfaces exposed by INFTY. It focuses on arguments, return values, and closure conventions rather than algorithm derivations.

docs.title: Imports
docs.text:
from infty import optim as infty_optim
from infty import plot as infty_plot

or:

from infty.optim import C_Flat, ZeroFlow, UniGrad_FS
from infty.plot import visualize_landscape, visualize_esd, visualize_conflicts, visualize_trajectory

docs.title: Closure contract
docs.text:
Most optimizer APIs expect a closure with this format:

def loss_fn():
    logits = model(inputs)
    loss_1 = criterion(logits, targets)
    return logits, [loss_1]

The returned value must be:

(logits, loss_list)

where loss_list is a list of scalar tensors.

docs.title: Public optimizer exports
docs.text: Class Family Description InftyBaseOptimizer Base Shared wrapper for geometry-aware optimizers. C_Flat Geometry reshaping Flatness-aware continual-learning optimizer. GAM Geometry reshaping Geometry-aware minimization optimizer. GSAM Geometry reshaping Generalized SAM-style optimizer. SAM Geometry reshaping Sharpness-aware minimization baseline. LookSAM Geometry reshaping Look-ahead SAM-style optimizer. ZeroFlow Zeroth-order updates Zeroth-order and forward-gradient optimizer. UniGrad_FS Gradient filtering Unified gradient projection with flatter sharpness. GradVac Gradient filtering Gradient vaccine-style conflict mitigation. OGD Gradient filtering Orthogonal gradient descent-style method. PCGrad Gradient filtering Projected conflicting gradients. CAGrad Gradient filtering Conflict-averse gradient method.

docs.title: InftyBaseOptimizer
docs.text:
InftyBaseOptimizer(
    params,
    base_optimizer,
    model,
    adaptive=False,
    perturb_eps=1e-12,
    grad_reduce="mean",
    **kwargs,
)

Base class for geometry-aware INFTY optimizers.

Parameter Description params Iterable of parameters to optimize. Usually model.parameters(). base_optimizer PyTorch optimizer instance that performs the final parameter update. model PyTorch model. Used for running-stat handling and distributed no_sync. adaptive Whether perturbations should be scaled adaptively by parameter magnitude. perturb_eps Numerical stabilizer for perturbation scaling. grad_reduce Distributed gradient reduction mode: "mean" or "sum".

Methods:

optimizer.set_closure(loss_fn)
optimizer.step(closure=None, delay=False)
optimizer.delay_step()
optimizer.post_process(train_loader=None)
optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=False)

docs.title: C_Flat
docs.text:
C_Flat(
    params,
    base_optimizer,
    model,
    args,
    adaptive=False,
    perturb_eps=1e-12,
    grad_reduce="mean",
    **kwargs,
)

Flatness-aware optimizer for continual-learning workflows.

args dictionary:

Key Default Description strategy "basic" Strategy variant. Supported values: "basic", "plus". rho 0.1 Perturbation radius. lamb 0.2 Gradient aggregation coefficient. rho_scheduler None Optional scheduler. A 5.0 Used by "plus" strategy. k 0.01 Used by "plus" strategy. t0 80 Used by "plus" strategy. cof 1.0 Used by "plus" strategy.

docs.title: ZeroFlow
docs.text:
ZeroFlow(params, base_optimizer, model, args, **kwargs)

Zeroth-order and forward-gradient optimizer.

args dictionary:

Key Default Description q 1 Number of random query directions. inftyopt "zo_sgd" Update variant. perturbation_mode "two_side" Finite-difference mode: "one_side" or "two_side". zo_eps 0.001 Perturbation scale. use_history_grad False Whether to smooth estimated directions. alpha 0.9 Smoothing coefficient. gradient_sparsity None Optional sparsity mask ratio or per-parameter dictionary. memory_efficient False Recompute random directions instead of storing them.

Supported inftyopt values:

zo_sgd
zo_adam
zo_sgd_sign
zo_adam_sign
zo_sgd_conserve
zo_adam_conserve
forward_grad

docs.title: UniGrad_FS
docs.text:
UniGrad_FS(params, base_optimizer, model, args, **kwargs)

Gradient-filtering optimizer for two-objective continual-learning updates.

args dictionary:

Key Default Description task_id 0 if omitted Current task index. utype "model-wise" "model-wise" or "layer-wise". k_idx [-1] Gradient block indices. S_T [0.1] Similarity threshold. beta 0.9 Threshold update coefficient. rho 0.05 Perturbation radius. perturb_eps 1e-12 Numerical stabilizer. adaptive False Adaptive perturbation flag.

For task_id > 0, the closure must return exactly two loss terms:

return logits, [old_loss, new_loss]

docs.title: infty.plot
docs.text:
Public exports:

visualize_loss_landscape
visualize_landscape
visualize_esd
visualize_conflicts
visualize_trajectory

visualize_landscape is an alias of visualize_loss_landscape.

docs.title: visualize_loss_landscape
docs.text:
visualize_loss_landscape(
    optimizer,
    model,
    create_loss_fn,
    loader,
    task,
    device,
    limit=0.3,
    samples=11,
    output_dir=None,
    dir_path=None,
)

Returns:

{
    "eigen_path": "...",
    "loss_path": "...",
    "plot_path": "...",
}

docs.title: visualize_esd
docs.text:
visualize_esd(
    optimizer,
    model,
    create_loss_fn,
    loader,
    task,
    device,
    output_dir=None,
    dir_path=None,
)

Returns:

{
    "trace_path": "...",
    "esd_path": "...",
    "plot_path": "...",
}

docs.title: visualize_conflicts
docs.text:
visualize_conflicts(
    optimizer,
    task=None,
    output_dir=None,
    task_id=None,
    dir_path=None,
)

The API only exports the top-level cosine-similarity curve and sim_list_task*.pt. The optimizer must expose sim_list or sim_arr, or you may pass sim_values / conflict_records.

docs.title: visualize_trajectory
docs.text:
visualize_trajectory(
    optimizer_name,
    init=None,
    n_iter=10000,
    lr=0.1,
    output_dir=None,
    grid_size=500,
)

Supported optimizer_name values:

sgd
adam
adamw
forward_grad
zo_sgd
zo_sgd_q4
zo_sgd_sign
zo_sgd_conserve
zo_adam
zo_adam_q4
zo_adam_sign
zo_adam_conserve

docs.title: Stability notes
docs.text: INFTY is currently an alpha-stage research library. Prefer named arguments and record the commit hash used in experiments.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: This page explains the structure of INFTY and the design choices behind the library.

docs.title: Module structure
docs.text:
src/infty/
├── optim/
│   ├── geometry_reshaping/
│   ├── gradient_filtering/
│   └── zeroth_order_updates/
├── plot/
└── utils/

docs.title: Geometry reshaping
docs.text:
Located under:

src/infty/optim/geometry_reshaping/

These optimizers modify local loss geometry or perturbation behavior. They generally inherit from InftyBaseOptimizer.

docs.title: Gradient filtering
docs.text:
Located under:

src/infty/optim/gradient_filtering/

These optimizers consume multiple losses and modify gradients to reduce objective conflicts. Shared utilities live in EasyCLMultiObjOptimizer.

docs.title: Zeroth-order updates
docs.text:
Located under:

src/infty/optim/zeroth_order_updates/

These optimizers estimate update directions through finite differences, random perturbations, or forward-gradient methods.

docs.title: Optimizer wrapper flow
docs.text:
The typical training flow is:

user training loop
  -> create closure
  -> optimizer.set_closure(loss_fn)
  -> optimizer.step()
       -> optimizer-specific gradient/perturbation logic
       -> base_optimizer.step()

This keeps the outer training loop simple while allowing each optimizer to control the number of closure evaluations and gradient operations.

docs.title: Closure format
docs.text:
The standard closure format is:

def loss_fn():
    logits = model(inputs)
    loss = criterion(logits, targets)
    return logits, [loss]

The use of loss_list rather than a single loss allows optimizers to distinguish objectives, such as old-task and new-task losses.

docs.title: State handling
docs.text:
Many optimizers store temporary tensors in self.state[p] for each parameter p. Common state entries include:

  • saved gradients;
  • perturbation vectors;
  • historical gradient estimates;
  • similarity values.

When adding new state entries, use descriptive names and ensure temporary perturbations are reverted before returning from step().

docs.title: Plotting architecture
docs.text:
Plotting utilities are pure diagnostic tools. They should:

  • accept an explicit output_dir;
  • restore model state after perturbing weights;
  • return saved artifact paths;
  • avoid hidden benchmark-specific assumptions.

docs.title: Public API exports
docs.text:
Public APIs should be exported through package-level __init__.py files:

src/infty/optim/__init__.py
src/infty/plot/__init__.py

This allows users to write stable imports such as:

from infty.optim import C_Flat
from infty.plot import visualize_landscape

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: This changelog records documentation and library-facing changes. For formal releases, use semantic versioning when possible.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Structured documentation homepage.
  • Installation guide.
  • Quick start guide.
  • User guide.
  • API reference.
  • Developer guide.
  • Examples page.
  • Troubleshooting page.
  • Architecture overview.
  • Citation guidance.
  • MkDocs configuration for GitHub Pages.
  • Optional Sphinx/RST scaffold for generated API documentation.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• INFTY is currently treated as alpha-stage research software.
  • Public optimizer signatures should be recorded in the API reference whenever they change.

docs.title: Citation
docs.text: If INFTY or its included algorithms are useful in your work, please cite the corresponding software and method papers.

docs.title: Software citation
docs.text:
For the toolkit, use a software citation similar to:

@software{infty_toolkit,
  title        = {INFTY: An Optimization Toolkit to Support Continual AI},
  author       = {INFTY contributors},
  year         = {2026},
  url          = {https://github.com/WanNaa/INFTY_demo},
  note         = {Research software, alpha release}
}

Replace the year, version, DOI, or official repository URL if a formal release is created.

docs.title: Algorithm papers
docs.text: INFTY currently exposes three optimizer families under infty.optim. The papers below are grouped by family, and each family is ordered by year from newest to oldest.

docs.title: Geometry reshaping
docs.text:
Year Method name Paper 2025 C-Flat++ C-Flat++: Towards a More Efficient and Powerful Framework for Continual Learning. 2024 C-Flat Make Continual Learning Stronger via C-Flat. 2023 GAM Gradient Norm Aware Minimization Seeks First-Order Flatness and Improves Generalization. 2022 LookSAM Towards Efficient and Scalable Sharpness-Aware Minimization. 2022 GSAM Surrogate Gap Minimization Improves Sharpness-Aware Training. 2021 SAM Sharpness-Aware Minimization for Efficiently Improving Generalization.

Implementation mapping: C_Flat exposes both C-Flat with strategy="basic" and C-Flat++ with strategy="plus".

docs.title: Zeroth-order updates
docs.text:
Year Method name Paper 2025 ZeroFlow Zeroflow: Overcoming Catastrophic Forgetting Is Easier Than You Think. 2023 ZeroFlow (MeZO-style variants) Fine-Tuning Language Models with Just Forward Passes. 2022 ZeroFlow (forward gradient) Gradients without Backpropagation.

Implementation mapping: ZeroFlow uses inftyopt="forward_grad" for the forward-gradient path, and uses zo_sgd, zo_adam, zo_sgd_sign, zo_adam_sign, zo_sgd_conserve, or zo_adam_conserve for the MeZO-style zeroth-order paths.

docs.title: Gradient filtering
docs.text:
Year Method name Paper 2024 UniGrad-FS UniGrad-FS: Unified Gradient Projection With Flatter Sharpness for Continual Learning. 2021 CAGrad Conflict-Averse Gradient Descent for Multi-Task Learning. 2021 GradVac Gradient Vaccine: Investigating and Improving Multi-task Optimization in Massively Multilingual Models. 2020 PCGrad Gradient Surgery for Multi-Task Learning. 2019 OGD Orthogonal Gradient Descent for Continual Learning.

For publications, cite the specific paper corresponding to the optimizer class or execution path used in your experiments.

docs.title: Reproducibility note
docs.text:
When citing INFTY in experiments, report:

  • INFTY version or commit hash;
  • optimizer name;
  • optimizer args dictionary;
  • base optimizer and hyperparameters;
  • PyTorch version;
  • benchmark and task order.

docs.title: Developer Guide
docs.text: This guide describes how to extend INFTY with new optimizers, plotting utilities, examples, and documentation.

docs.title: Repository layout
docs.text:
A typical INFTY repository layout is:

INFTY_demo/
├── README.md
├── pyproject.toml
├── setup.py
├── src/
│   └── infty/
│       ├── optim/
│       │   ├── geometry_reshaping/
│       │   ├── gradient_filtering/
│       │   └── zeroth_order_updates/
│       ├── plot/
│       └── utils/
├── examples/
│   └── infty_minimal/
├── workdirs/
│   └── PILOT/
├── tests/
│   ├── optim/
│   └── plot/
└── docs/

docs.title: Development installation
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/WanNaa/INFTY_demo.git
cd INFTY_demo
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install -e .[examples]

Run tests:

PYTEST_DISABLE_PLUGIN_AUTOLOAD=1 pytest tests

Build documentation:

python -m pip install -r requirements-docs.txt
mkdocs build --strict

docs.title: 1. Keep the PyTorch optimizer mental model
docs.text: INFTY should feel like an extension of PyTorch optimizers. Users should be able to keep their existing parameter groups and base optimizer settings.

docs.title: 2. Use closures for optimizer-specific gradient logic
docs.text: The outer training loop should not need to know how many backward passes or perturbation passes an optimizer uses. That logic belongs inside the optimizer.

docs.title: 3. Keep research algorithms modular
docs.text: Do not place benchmark-specific assumptions inside library code. Benchmark logic belongs in examples/ or experiment scripts.

docs.title: 4. Make diagnostics reproducible
docs.text: Plotting functions should return output paths and save artifacts under deterministic directories.

docs.title: Adding a geometry reshaping optimizer
docs.text:
Use this family when the optimizer modifies the local geometry, perturbation direction, or flatness-aware gradient.

  1. Add a new file under:
src/infty/optim/geometry_reshaping/
  1. Inherit from InftyBaseOptimizer when possible.
from .base import InftyBaseOptimizer

class MyOptimizer(InftyBaseOptimizer):
    def __init__(self, params, base_optimizer, model, args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(params, base_optimizer, model, **kwargs)
        self.name = "my_optimizer"
        self.args = args

    def step(self, closure=None, delay=False):
        if closure:
            get_grad = closure
        else:
            get_grad = self.forward_backward_func
        logits, loss_list = get_grad()
        self.base_optimizer.step()
        return logits, loss_list
  1. Export it from src/infty/optim/__init__.py:
from .geometry_reshaping.my_optimizer import MyOptimizer

__all__ = [
    ...,
    "MyOptimizer",
]
  1. Add tests and documentation.

docs.title: Adding a gradient filtering optimizer
docs.text:
Use this family when the optimizer consumes multiple losses and modifies their gradients.

  1. Add a file under:
src/infty/optim/gradient_filtering/
  1. Inherit from EasyCLMultiObjOptimizer when the optimizer needs flattened gradient utilities.
from infty.optim.gradient_filtering.base import EasyCLMultiObjOptimizer

class MyMultiObjectiveOptimizer(EasyCLMultiObjOptimizer):
    def __init__(self, params, base_optimizer, model, args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(params, base_optimizer, model, **kwargs)
        self.name = "my_multi_obj"
        self.args = args

    def step(self, closure=None, delay=False):
        get_grad = closure if closure else self.forward_func
        logits, loss_list = get_grad()
        self._compute_grad_dim()
        grads = self._compute_grad(loss_list, mode="backward")
        new_grads = grads.sum(0)
        self._reset_grad(new_grads)
        if not delay:
            self.base_optimizer.step()
        return logits, loss_list
  1. Export it from src/infty/optim/__init__.py.
  2. Add a minimal two-loss test.

docs.title: Adding a zeroth-order optimizer
docs.text:
Use this family when the optimizer estimates update directions without ordinary loss.backward().

  1. Add a file under:
src/infty/optim/zeroth_order_updates/
  1. Ensure that parameter perturbations are restored correctly after finite-difference evaluation.
  2. Avoid accumulating stale gradients.
  3. Document whether the method uses torch.no_grad(), torch.inference_mode(), or forward-mode AD.

docs.title: Adding a plotting utility
docs.text:
1. Add the function under src/infty/plot/.
  2. Make output_dir configurable.
  3. Return a dictionary or string containing saved artifact paths.
  4. Restore model state if the function perturbs model weights.
  5. Export the function in src/infty/plot/__init__.py.
  6. Add a small test that verifies the function saves an artifact on a tiny model or toy problem.

docs.title: Testing guidelines
docs.text:
Recommended minimal tests:

  • import tests for public APIs;
  • one-step optimizer tests on a tiny neural network;
  • closure-shape tests;
  • serialization tests for wrapped base optimizers;
  • plot regression tests using small grid sizes.

Example optimizer regression test:

def test_cflat_one_step():
    model = torch.nn.Linear(4, 2)
    inputs = torch.randn(8, 4)
    targets = torch.randint(0, 2, (8,))
    base = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01)
    opt = C_Flat(model.parameters(), base, model, args={"rho": 0.01})

    def loss_fn():
        logits = model(inputs)
        loss = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits, targets)
        return logits, [loss]

    opt.set_closure(loss_fn)
    logits, losses = opt.step()
    assert len(losses) == 1

docs.title: Documentation rules
docs.text:
Every public optimizer should have:

  • a short conceptual description in docs/user_guide.md;
  • constructor arguments in docs/api_reference.md;
  • at least one runnable example or test;
  • notes about closure requirements;
  • notes about task-specific assumptions.

docs.title: Release checklist
docs.text:
Before releasing a new version:

  • [ ] run tests;
  • [ ] build MkDocs documentation with mkdocs build --strict;
  • [ ] build optional Sphinx docs if RST API pages are used;
  • [ ] update docs/changelog.md;
  • [ ] update version metadata;
  • [ ] verify PyPI package metadata and homepage URL;
  • [ ] verify README documentation links;
  • [ ] tag the release;
  • [ ] archive experiment-relevant commit hashes for paper reproducibility.

docs.title: API stability
docs.text: The project is currently alpha-stage. If a public constructor or closure convention changes, document it in the changelog and provide a migration note.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: This page lists runnable examples and recommended experiment entry points.

docs.title: PILOT demo
docs.text:
The repository includes a PILOT-based example under workdirs/PILOT.

Install optional dependencies:

cd INFTY_demo
python -m pip install .[examples]

Run C-Flat:

cd workdirs/PILOT
python main.py --config=exps/memo_scr.json --inftyopt=c_flat --workdir ../results/memo_cflat

Run ZeroFlow conservative update:

python main.py --config=exps/ease.json --inftyopt=zo_sgd_conserve --workdir ../results/ease_zo

Run UniGrad-FS:

python main.py --config=exps/icarl.json --inftyopt=unigrad_fs --workdir ../results/icarl_unigrad_fs

docs.title: Minimal examples included in this documentation package
docs.text:
This documentation package also contains small standalone scripts under:

examples/infty_minimal/
File Purpose minimal_cflat.py One-step/small-loop C-Flat example on toy data. minimal_zeroflow.py ZeroFlow example using a finite-difference update. minimal_unigrad_fs.py Two-loss UniGrad-FS example. minimal_visualization.py Toy trajectory visualization example.

Run them from the repository root after installing INFTY:

python examples/infty_minimal/minimal_cflat.py
python examples/infty_minimal/minimal_zeroflow.py
python examples/infty_minimal/minimal_unigrad_fs.py
python examples/infty_minimal/minimal_visualization.py

docs.title: Writing your own example
docs.text:
A good example should include:

  1. environment assumptions;
  2. dataset or toy-data creation;
  3. base optimizer construction;
  4. INFTY optimizer construction;
  5. closure definition;
  6. set_closure and step calls;
  7. expected output or artifact path.

docs.title: Example: C-Flat integration pattern
docs.text:
base_optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01, momentum=0.9)
optimizer = infty_optim.C_Flat(
    params=model.parameters(),
    base_optimizer=base_optimizer,
    model=model,
    args={"rho": 0.05, "lamb": 0.2},
)

for inputs, targets in loader:
    optimizer.set_closure(make_loss_fn(inputs, targets))
    logits, loss_list = optimizer.step()

docs.title: Example: loss-landscape visualization pattern
docs.text:
from infty import plot as infty_plot

result = infty_plot.visualize_landscape(
    optimizer=optimizer,
    model=model,
    create_loss_fn=create_loss_fn,
    loader=train_loader,
    task=task_id,
    device=device,
    output_dir="workdirs/plots/diagnostics/landscape/demo",
)
print(result["plot_path"])

docs.title: Example: conflict visualization pattern
docs.text:
from infty import plot as infty_plot

result = infty_plot.visualize_conflicts(
    optimizer=optimizer,
    task=task_id,
    output_dir="workdirs/plots/diagnostics/conflicts/demo",
)
print(result)

docs.title: Base optimizer
docs.text: A standard PyTorch optimizer, such as torch.optim.SGD or torch.optim.Adam, wrapped by an INFTY optimizer.

docs.title: Closure
docs.text: A function supplied by the user that re-runs the model forward pass and returns (logits, loss_list).

docs.title: Geometry reshaping
docs.text: An optimizer family that modifies local loss geometry or flatness behavior through perturbation and gradient aggregation.

docs.title: Gradient filtering
docs.text: An optimizer family that modifies gradients from multiple objectives to reduce interference.

docs.title: Zeroth-order update
docs.text: An update that estimates a descent direction without ordinary reverse-mode backpropagation.

docs.title: Loss list
docs.text: A list of scalar tensors returned by a closure. Single-objective training uses one loss. Multi-objective training may use multiple losses.

docs.title: Similarity threshold
docs.text: A threshold used by gradient-filtering methods to decide whether objectives are sufficiently aligned.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This page explains how to install INFTY for normal use, example execution, and local development.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
INFTY is a Python package built around PyTorch. The package metadata declares the following core dependencies:

  • torch
  • torchvision
  • numpy
  • matplotlib
  • scipy
  • seaborn
  • tqdm

Although the package metadata allows Python >=3.6, a modern Python environment such as Python 3.8--3.11 is recommended because current PyTorch releases are usually tested against newer Python versions.

docs.title: Install from PyPI
docs.text:
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install infty

Verify the installation:

python -c "from infty.optim import C_Flat, ZeroFlow, UniGrad_FS; print('INFTY import succeeded')"

docs.title: Install from source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/WanNaa/INFTY_demo.git
cd INFTY_demo
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install .

For editable development:

python -m pip install -e .

docs.title: Install example dependencies
docs.text:
Some examples require optional packages such as easydict, Pillow, PyYAML, scikit-learn, timm, and umap-learn.

python -m pip install .[examples]

For editable development with examples:

python -m pip install -e .[examples]

docs.title: Recommended conda environment
docs.text:
conda create -n infty python=3.10
conda activate infty
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install infty

If you need a specific CUDA build of PyTorch, install PyTorch first using the command matching your CUDA/runtime setup, then install INFTY.

docs.title: Documentation dependencies
docs.text:
To build the documentation website locally:

python -m pip install -r requirements-docs.txt
mkdocs serve

To build the optional Sphinx/RST documentation:

python -m pip install -r requirements-docs.txt
sphinx-build -b html docs/sphinx docs/sphinx/_build/html

docs.title: Common checks
docs.text:
Check that infty is imported from the expected location:

python -c "import infty; print(infty.__file__)"

Check optimizer exports:

python -c "from infty.optim import C_Flat, ZeroFlow, UniGrad_FS; print(C_Flat, ZeroFlow, UniGrad_FS)"

Check plotting exports:

python -c "from infty.plot import visualize_landscape, visualize_esd, visualize_conflicts, visualize_trajectory; print('plot utilities imported')"

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: If installation fails, see Troubleshooting. The most common causes are incompatible PyTorch/CUDA versions, installing into the wrong Python environment, or missing optional example dependencies.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This page shows the minimum patterns needed to use INFTY optimizers in a PyTorch training loop.

docs.title: Core pattern
docs.text:
Most INFTY optimizers use the same pattern:

  1. create a normal PyTorch optimizer;
  2. wrap it with an INFTY optimizer;
  3. write a closure that returns (logits, loss_list);
  4. call optimizer.set_closure(loss_fn);
  5. call optimizer.step().

The closure contract is important:

logits, loss_list = loss_fn()

where loss_list must be a list of scalar tensors.

docs.title: Minimal C-Flat example
docs.text:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset
from infty.optim import C_Flat

x = torch.randn(64, 16)
y = torch.randint(0, 4, (64,))
loader = DataLoader(TensorDataset(x, y), batch_size=16, shuffle=True)

model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(16, 32), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(32, 4))
base_optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01, momentum=0.9)

optimizer = C_Flat(
    params=model.parameters(),
    base_optimizer=base_optimizer,
    model=model,
    args={"rho": 0.05, "lamb": 0.2, "strategy": "basic"},
)

def make_loss_fn(inputs, targets):
    def loss_fn():
        logits = model(inputs)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, targets)
        return logits, [loss]
    return loss_fn

model.train()
for inputs, targets in loader:
    optimizer.set_closure(make_loss_fn(inputs, targets))
    logits, loss_list = optimizer.step()
    print(float(sum(loss_list)))

docs.title: Minimal ZeroFlow example
docs.text:
ZeroFlow estimates update directions without ordinary backpropagation. It still uses the same high-level closure format.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from infty.optim import ZeroFlow

model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(8, 16), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(16, 3))
inputs = torch.randn(32, 8)
targets = torch.randint(0, 3, (32,))

base_optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
optimizer = ZeroFlow(
    params=model.parameters(),
    base_optimizer=base_optimizer,
    model=model,
    args={
        "inftyopt": "zo_sgd",
        "q": 1,
        "zo_eps": 1e-3,
        "perturbation_mode": "two_side",
    },
)

def loss_fn():
    logits = model(inputs)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, targets)
    return logits, [loss]

optimizer.set_closure(loss_fn)
logits, loss_list = optimizer.step()
print(float(sum(loss_list)))

docs.title: Minimal UniGrad-FS example
docs.text:
UniGrad-FS is intended for two-objective continual-learning settings. For non-first tasks, it expects exactly two loss terms.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from infty.optim import UniGrad_FS

model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(10, 20), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(20, 5))
inputs = torch.randn(32, 10)
new_targets = torch.randint(0, 5, (32,))
old_targets = torch.randint(0, 5, (32,))

base_optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01)
optimizer = UniGrad_FS(
    params=model.parameters(),
    base_optimizer=base_optimizer,
    model=model,
    args={
        "task_id": 1,
        "utype": "model-wise",
        "S_T": [0.1],
        "beta": 0.9,
        "rho": 0.05,
    },
)

def loss_fn():
    logits = model(inputs)
    old_loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, old_targets)
    new_loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, new_targets)
    return logits, [old_loss, new_loss]

optimizer.set_closure(loss_fn)
logits, loss_list = optimizer.step()
print([float(loss) for loss in loss_list])

docs.title: Run the PILOT demo
docs.text:
cd INFTY_demo
python -m pip install .[examples]
cd workdirs/PILOT
python main.py --config=exps/memo_scr.json --inftyopt=c_flat --workdir ../results/memo_cflat
python main.py --config=exps/ease.json --inftyopt=zo_sgd_conserve --workdir ../results/ease_zo
python main.py --config=exps/icarl.json --inftyopt=unigrad_fs --workdir ../results/icarl_unigrad_fs

docs.title: Quick visualization example
docs.text:
from infty.plot import visualize_trajectory

visualize_trajectory(
    optimizer_name="adam",
    n_iter=500,
    lr=0.1,
    output_dir="workdirs/plots/diagnostics/trajectory/demo",
    grid_size=200,
)

docs.title: Release Checklist
docs.text: Use this checklist before publishing a new INFTY package or paper artifact.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
• [ ] All tests pass.
  • [ ] Public APIs import correctly.
  • [ ] Optimizer regression tests run on CPU.
  • [ ] Plot regression tests run with small grids.
  • [ ] Example scripts run or are clearly marked as requiring benchmark data.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• [ ] README links to docs/.
  • [ ] docs/user_guide.md reflects current optimizer behavior.
  • [ ] docs/api_reference.md reflects current public APIs.
  • [ ] docs/developer_guide.md describes extension workflow.
  • [ ] docs/troubleshooting.md includes known issues.
  • [ ] MkDocs builds with mkdocs build --strict.
  • [ ] Optional Sphinx docs build if used.

docs.title: Packaging
docs.text:
• [ ] Package version is updated.
  • [ ] Homepage URL is correct.
  • [ ] Dependencies are accurate.
  • [ ] Optional example dependencies are accurate.
  • [ ] License metadata is correct.
  • [ ] PyPI trusted publishing is configured for the GitHub pypi environment.
  • [ ] Release tag follows the v* pattern expected by .github/workflows/release.yml.
  • [ ] Release tag version matches the package version embedded in the built distribution.

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text:
• [ ] Release commit hash is recorded.
  • [ ] Experiment configs are archived.
  • [ ] Random seeds and task orders are documented.
  • [ ] Citation information is updated.

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text: This roadmap lists recommended improvements for making INFTY more robust as a public research library.

docs.title: Documentation roadmap
docs.text:
• [x] Add user guide.
  • [x] Add API reference.
  • [x] Add developer guide.
  • [x] Add troubleshooting page.
  • [x] Add examples page.
  • [x] Add MkDocs configuration.
  • [x] Add optional Sphinx/RST scaffold.
  • [ ] Add method-specific tutorials for each optimizer.
  • [ ] Add benchmark-specific reproduction guides.
  • [ ] Add generated API pages from docstrings.

docs.title: Engineering roadmap
docs.text:
• [ ] Add one-step optimizer tests for every public optimizer.
  • [ ] Add import tests for infty.optim and infty.plot.
  • [ ] Add continuous integration for tests.
  • [ ] Add documentation build checks.
  • [ ] Verify package metadata and homepage URLs before release.
  • [ ] Add release tags and changelog entries.

docs.title: Research usability roadmap
docs.text:
• [ ] Provide recommended hyperparameter ranges for each optimizer.
  • [ ] Provide benchmark-specific configs for common continual-learning datasets.
  • [ ] Add reproducibility checklists for paper experiments.
  • [ ] Add expected runtime and memory notes for each optimizer family.
  • [ ] Add ablation scripts for flatness, conflict, and zeroth-order components.

docs.title: API roadmap
docs.text:
• [ ] Stabilize constructor signatures across optimizer families.
  • [ ] Add consistent docstrings to every public class.
  • [ ] Add type hints where practical.
  • [ ] Add migration notes when public APIs change.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: This page collects common issues when installing INFTY, writing closures, running optimizers, and generating plots.

docs.title: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'infty'
docs.text:
The package is not installed in the active Python environment.

Check the active Python executable:

which python
python -m pip show infty

Install or reinstall:

python -m pip install -e .

docs.title: ModuleNotFoundError for optional example dependencies
docs.text:
The PILOT demo and some examples require optional dependencies.

python -m pip install .[examples]

docs.title: Optimizer does not update parameters
docs.text:
Check that:

  • model parameters have requires_grad=True;
  • the base optimizer was initialized with the same parameters;
  • the closure returns scalar losses;
  • optimizer.set_closure(loss_fn) is called before optimizer.step();
  • you are not accidentally calling base_optimizer.zero_grad() after optimizer.step() in a way that hides the update.

docs.title: Closure returns the wrong format
docs.text:
INFTY expects:

return logits, [loss]

not:

return loss

and not:

return logits, loss

For multi-objective optimizers, use:

return logits, [loss_1, loss_2]

docs.title: UniGrad_FS only supports two losses
docs.text:
For task_id > 0, UniGrad_FS requires exactly two losses:

return logits, [old_loss, new_loss]

If you have more than two loss terms, combine them before returning:

old_loss = replay_loss + regularization_loss
new_loss = supervised_loss
return logits, [old_loss, new_loss]

docs.title: C-Flat or SAM-style optimizer is slow
docs.text:
Geometry-aware optimizers may call the closure multiple times per step. This is expected because they evaluate perturbed model states.

Mitigations:

  • reduce batch size;
  • use mixed precision where safe;
  • use smaller models for debugging;
  • start with fewer epochs;
  • profile closure runtime.

docs.title: CUDA out of memory
docs.text:
Common causes:

  • large model and large batch;
  • optimizer calls closure multiple times;
  • visualization computes Hessian-related quantities;
  • graph retained by multi-objective gradient computation.

Mitigations:

base_optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)

and reduce:

  • batch size;
  • visualization samples;
  • trajectory grid_size;
  • number of zeroth-order queries q.

docs.title: BatchNorm behaves unexpectedly
docs.text:
Some geometry-aware optimizers temporarily disable or enable running statistics during perturbation passes. If results look unstable:

  • verify model train/eval mode;
  • use sufficiently large batches for BatchNorm;
  • consider freezing BatchNorm statistics in continual-learning stages;
  • compare with a base optimizer baseline.

docs.title: Distributed training issue with gradient reduction
docs.text:
InftyBaseOptimizer supports distributed gradient synchronization through grad_reduce.

Valid values:

"mean"
"sum"

If using DistributedDataParallel, ensure the model object passed into the optimizer is the DDP-wrapped model when no_sync() behavior is required.

docs.title: ZeroFlow produces unstable loss
docs.text:
Try:

  • smaller learning rate;
  • smaller or larger zo_eps depending on scale;
  • perturbation_mode="two_side";
  • q > 1 for lower-variance estimates;
  • use_history_grad=True;
  • zo_sgd_conserve or zo_adam_conserve.

docs.title: forward_grad path fails
docs.text: The forward_grad variant uses a functional-call and JVP path. It is more sensitive to the exact objective signature. Start with the standard zo_sgd mode, then migrate to forward_grad after confirming the closure and batch structure.

docs.title: Loss landscape or ESD visualization is slow
docs.text:
These utilities compute Hessian-related quantities and can be expensive.

For debugging:

visualize_landscape(..., samples=11, limit=0.3)
visualize_trajectory(..., n_iter=200, grid_size=100)

docs.title: Plot output is not found
docs.text:
All plotting functions accept output_dir. Pass an explicit directory:

output_dir="workdirs/plots/custom/my_experiment"

Most plotting functions return a dictionary or path containing the saved artifact location. Print the return value.

docs.title: Matplotlib fails on a headless server
docs.text:
Use a non-interactive backend before importing plotting code:

import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")

docs.title: How to report a bug
docs.text:
Include:

  • INFTY version or commit hash;
  • Python version;
  • PyTorch version;
  • CUDA version;
  • optimizer name and args dictionary;
  • base optimizer settings;
  • minimal closure code;
  • full error traceback.

docs.title: User Guide
docs.text: This guide explains how to use INFTY in continual-learning experiments and custom PyTorch training pipelines.

docs.title: What INFTY provides
docs.text:
INFTY exposes two main types of functionality:

  1. Optimizers under infty.optim;
  2. Visualization utilities under infty.plot.

The optimizer module contains three algorithm families:

Family Main purpose Public classes Geometry reshaping Improve flatness/generalization and reshape local loss geometry. C_Flat, SAM, GSAM, GAM, LookSAM Zeroth-order updates Estimate update directions without standard backward gradients. ZeroFlow Gradient filtering Mitigate interference among objectives or tasks. UniGrad_FS, GradVac, OGD, PCGrad, CAGrad

docs.title: Optimizer wrapper model
docs.text:
INFTY optimizers usually wrap a base PyTorch optimizer.

base_optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01, momentum=0.9)
optimizer = infty_optim.C_Flat(
    params=model.parameters(),
    base_optimizer=base_optimizer,
    model=model,
    args={"rho": 0.05, "lamb": 0.2},
)

The base optimizer controls learning rate, parameter groups, weight decay, momentum, and other ordinary PyTorch optimizer settings. The INFTY wrapper controls the continual-learning or geometry-aware update logic.

docs.title: Closure contract
docs.text:
A valid closure returns:

return logits, loss_list

where:

  • logits is the model output or a task-specific output object;
  • loss_list is a list of scalar PyTorch tensors;
  • sum(loss_list) should be the total training objective unless the optimizer explicitly consumes separate losses.

docs.title: Single-objective closure
docs.text:
def make_loss_fn(inputs, targets):
    def loss_fn():
        logits = model(inputs)
        loss = criterion(logits, targets)
        return logits, [loss]
    return loss_fn

docs.title: Multi-objective closure
docs.text:
def make_loss_fn(inputs, new_targets, replay_targets):
    def loss_fn():
        logits = model(inputs)
        old_task_loss = criterion(logits, replay_targets)
        new_task_loss = criterion(logits, new_targets)
        return logits, [old_task_loss, new_task_loss]
    return loss_fn

For UniGrad_FS, non-first tasks require exactly two losses: one old-task objective and one new-task objective.

docs.title: Training-loop pattern
docs.text:
for task_id, task_loader in enumerate(task_loaders):
    model.train()
    for inputs, targets in task_loader:
        inputs, targets = inputs.to(device), targets.to(device)
        loss_fn = make_loss_fn(inputs, targets)
        optimizer.set_closure(loss_fn)
        logits, loss_list = optimizer.step()

Do not call loss.backward() outside the closure unless the optimizer documentation explicitly instructs otherwise.

docs.title: Geometry reshaping optimizers
docs.text: Geometry reshaping methods are useful when the goal is to encourage flatter or more stable local loss geometry across continual-learning tasks.

docs.title: C-Flat
docs.text:
C_Flat is the main flatness-aware optimizer exposed by INFTY.

optimizer = infty_optim.C_Flat(
    params=model.parameters(),
    base_optimizer=base_optimizer,
    model=model,
    args={
        "strategy": "basic",
        "rho": 0.05,
        "lamb": 0.2,
    },
)

Important arguments:

Argument Meaning strategy "basic" or "plus". rho Perturbation radius. lamb Aggregation coefficient. rho_scheduler Optional scheduler for perturbation radius. adaptive Whether to use adaptive perturbations scaled by parameter magnitude.

docs.title: SAM, GSAM, GAM, LookSAM
docs.text: These classes expose related geometry-aware optimization strategies. They follow the same wrapping logic: create a base optimizer, pass model parameters and the model, set a closure, then call step().

docs.title: Zeroth-order updates
docs.text:
ZeroFlow supports zeroth-order and forward-gradient update variants. It is intended for settings where ordinary backpropagation is expensive, unavailable, or undesirable.

optimizer = infty_optim.ZeroFlow(
    params=model.parameters(),
    base_optimizer=base_optimizer,
    model=model,
    args={
        "inftyopt": "zo_sgd",
        "q": 1,
        "zo_eps": 1e-3,
        "perturbation_mode": "two_side",
        "memory_efficient": False,
    },
)

Supported inftyopt values include:

Value Description zo_sgd Zeroth-order update with SGD-style base optimizer. zo_adam Zeroth-order update with Adam-style base optimizer. zo_sgd_sign Sign version of the zeroth-order update. zo_adam_sign Sign version with Adam-style base optimizer. zo_sgd_conserve Conservative update that rejects harmful moves. zo_adam_conserve Conservative update with Adam-style base optimizer. forward_grad Forward-gradient update path.

Important arguments:

Argument Meaning q Number of zeroth-order query directions. zo_eps Finite-difference perturbation size. perturbation_mode "one_side" or "two_side". use_history_grad Whether to smooth estimated directions over time. alpha Smoothing coefficient for historical directions. gradient_sparsity Optional float or dictionary controlling random sparsity masks. memory_efficient Recompute noise instead of storing it.

docs.title: Gradient filtering optimizers
docs.text: Gradient filtering methods are useful when multiple objectives interfere with each other, which is common in replay-based or multi-loss continual learning.

docs.title: UniGrad-FS
docs.text:
UniGrad_FS handles gradient projection with a flatter-sharpness mechanism. For tasks after the first task, it expects exactly two objective terms.

optimizer = infty_optim.UniGrad_FS(
    params=model.parameters(),
    base_optimizer=base_optimizer,
    model=model,
    args={
        "task_id": task_id,
        "utype": "model-wise",
        "S_T": [0.1],
        "beta": 0.9,
        "rho": 0.05,
    },
)

Important arguments:

Argument Meaning task_id Current task index. Task 0 uses ordinary summed-loss behavior. utype "model-wise" or "layer-wise" gradient handling. S_T Similarity threshold. A scalar list such as [0.1] is accepted for model-wise mode. beta Threshold update coefficient. rho Perturbation radius used by the flatter-sharpness component.

docs.title: PCGrad, CAGrad, GradVac, OGD
docs.text: These optimizers follow the same multi-objective wrapper style. They are useful baselines or alternatives when analyzing task-gradient interference.

docs.title: Loss landscape
docs.text:
from infty import plot as infty_plot

infty_plot.visualize_landscape(
    optimizer=optimizer,
    model=model,
    create_loss_fn=create_loss_fn,
    loader=train_loader,
    task=task_id,
    device=device,
    output_dir="workdirs/plots/diagnostics/landscape/demo",
)

docs.title: Hessian ESD
docs.text:
infty_plot.visualize_esd(
    optimizer=optimizer,
    model=model,
    create_loss_fn=create_loss_fn,
    loader=train_loader,
    task=task_id,
    device=device,
    output_dir="workdirs/plots/diagnostics/esd/demo",
)

docs.title: Conflict curves
docs.text:
infty_plot.visualize_conflicts(
    optimizer=optimizer,
    task=task_id,
    output_dir="workdirs/plots/diagnostics/conflicts/demo",
)

This API only renders the top-level similarity curve. The optimizer must expose sim_list or sim_arr. UniGrad_FS records similarity values through sim_list.

docs.title: Optimization trajectory
docs.text:
infty_plot.visualize_trajectory(
    optimizer_name="adam",
    output_dir="workdirs/plots/diagnostics/trajectory/demo",
)

docs.title: Choosing an optimizer
docs.text: Situation Suggested starting point Need a flatness-aware baseline C_Flat or SAM Need continual-learning flatness behavior C_Flat Need backprop-free or gradient-free updates ZeroFlow Need old/new task objective balancing UniGrad_FS Need multi-objective baselines PCGrad, CAGrad, GradVac, OGD Need diagnostics only infty.plot

docs.title: Reproducibility recommendations
docs.text:
For every experiment, record:

  • INFTY version or commit hash;
  • PyTorch version;
  • optimizer class and args dictionary;
  • base optimizer and its hyperparameters;
  • random seed;
  • task order and dataset split;
  • output directory for plot artifacts.
